title: A History of English Prose Fiction
description: A History of English Prose Fiction — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A History of English Prose Fiction" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Allegory
memes.description: 'A narrative form in which characters, events, and settings represent abstract ideas or moral qualities, conveying a hidden meaning beneath the literal story.'

memes.label: Amelia Opie
memes.description: 'English novelist (1769–1853), author of "Simple Tales" and "Tales of Real Life," noted for natural pathos and delicacy of sentiment in the domestic fiction tradition.'

memes.label: America
memes.description: 'America as a market, subject, and source of literary influence for English prose fiction, from Cooper and Irving through Hawthorne and Stowe.'

memes.label: Ann Radcliffe
memes.description: 'English Gothic novelist, author of "The Mysteries of Udolpho" (1794), celebrated for her skill in depicting supernatural terror explained by natural causes.'

memes.label: Anthony Trollope
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist (1815–1882), author of the Barsetshire and Palliser series, celebrated for his faithful depiction of English social life, clerical characters, and the workings of parliamentary politics.'

memes.label: Aphra Behn
memes.description: 'English novelist, playwright, poet, and spy; author of Oroonoko (1688), one of the earliest English novels and a pioneering work of anti-slavery fiction.'

memes.label: Baroness Tautphoeus
memes.description: 'Anglo-Irish novelist (1807–1893) who wrote fascinating novels of English and German life including Quits, At Odds, and The Initials.'

memes.label: Bret Harte
memes.description: 'American author known for his original and graphic short stories of California mining camp life, which opened a new vein of romance in American fiction.'

memes.label: Charlemagne
memes.description: 'Frankish king and emperor whose legends formed one of the three great cycles of medieval chivalric romance, alongside the Arthurian and classical traditions.'

memes.label: Charles Brockden Brown
memes.description: 'The first significant American novelist, active at the close of the 18th century.'

memes.label: Charles Dickens
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist; author of "The Pickwick Papers" (1836–37), "Oliver Twist" (1837–39), "David Copperfield" (1849–50), and other works—the most popular English novelist of the nineteenth century.'

memes.label: Charles Kingsley
memes.description: 'Victorian clergyman, novelist, and social reformer, author of "Hypatia" (1853), "Westward Ho!" (1855), and "Alton Locke" (1850), known for blending didactic purpose with fiction.'

memes.label: Charles Lever
memes.description: "Irish novelist known for about thirty jovial, dashing novels of Irish life; creator of Charles O'Malley, the Irish Dragoon."

memes.label: Charles Maturin
memes.description: 'Irish Gothic novelist and clergyman, author of The Family of Montorio and Melmoth the Wanderer, associated with the sensational extreme of the Radcliffe school.'

memes.label: Charles Reade
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist and playwright who combined popular storytelling with social criticism; author of "It Is Never Too Late to Mend" and "The Cloister and the Hearth."'

memes.label: Charlotte Lennox
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist and poet; author of The Female Quixote (1752), an entertaining satire on French romances praised by Samuel Johnson and Henry Fielding.'

memes.label: Charlotte Yonge
memes.description: 'English novelist (1823–1901), author of "The Heir of Redclyffe," a significant contributor to the English domestic fiction tradition.'

memes.label: Chivalry
memes.description: 'The medieval code of knightly conduct combining military prowess, courtly love, and Christian piety, forming the spiritual foundation of chivalric romance.'

memes.label: Clara Reeve
memes.description: 'English Gothic novelist, author of "The Old English Baron" (1778) and "The Progress of Romance" (1785), a key figure in the late 18th-century romantic revival in English fiction.'

memes.label: Daniel Defoe
memes.description: 'English novelist, journalist, and pamphleteer whose realistic narratives, especially Robinson Crusoe, laid the foundation for the modern English novel.'

memes.label: Didactic Purpose in Fiction
memes.description: 'The recurring characteristic of English prose fiction in which narrative serves a didactic function—instructing, reforming, or morally educating the reader.'

memes.label: Domestic Novel
memes.description: 'A subgenre of fiction concerned with family life, household relationships, and the everyday experience of private domestic existence, particularly developed by women novelists in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.'

memes.label: Edgar Allan Poe
memes.description: 'American writer known for tales of mystery and the macabre; author of "Tales of the Grotesque and the Arabesque"'

memes.label: Edward Bulwer-Lytton
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist and politician who studied and developed the novel as a form of literature, excelling in every department of fiction from historical romance to fashionable and criminal novels.'

memes.label: Eliza Haywood
memes.description: Early 18th-century English novelist; one of the earliest female novelists alongside Aphra Behn and Delarivier Manley; author of the early domestic novel Miss Betsey Thoughtless

memes.label: Elizabeth Gaskell
memes.description: 'English novelist and biographer; author of Mary Barton (1848), a powerful novel of purpose depicting the trials of the manufacturing classes, and the first biography of Charlotte Brontë.'

memes.label: Elizabeth Inchbald
memes.description: 'English novelist, playwright, and actress; author of A Simple Story (1791) and Nature and Art (1796), noted for their pathetic power and emotional sincerity.'

memes.label: Elizabethan Literature
memes.description: 'The literary culture of the Elizabethan age (1558–1603), a period of extraordinary intellectual and material progress that laid the foundations for modern English prose fiction.'

memes.label: England
memes.description: 'The primary geographic and cultural setting for the development of English prose fiction, whose social evolution shaped every phase of the novel from the Restoration onward.'

memes.label: English Novel
memes.description: 'The tradition of English-language prose fiction from its 18th-century origins through Victorian development, encompassing diverse styles and concerns.'

memes.label: Euphuism
memes.description: 'An elaborate, antithetical prose style popularized by John Lyly''s "Euphues" (1579), characterized by balanced phrases, classical allusions, and rhetorical ornamentation.'

memes.label: France
memes.description: 'A major source of literary influence on English prose fiction, from medieval romance through the heroic romances of the seventeenth century.'

memes.label: Frances Burney
memes.description: 'English novelist, diarist, and playwright; author of Evelina (1778), a landmark novel of manners that influenced Jane Austen and the development of the English domestic novel.'

memes.label: Frances Trollope
memes.description: 'English novelist (1779–1863), mother of Anthony Trollope, who contributed largely to the English domestic novel in the early nineteenth century.'

memes.label: Frederick Marryat
memes.description: 'English naval novelist; author of about thirty sea-faring tales including Peter Simple and Midshipman Easy, celebrated as the head of maritime fiction in England.'

memes.label: George Eliot
memes.description: 'Pen name of Mary Ann Evans (1819–1880), the greatest female novelist in the English language, author of "Middlemarch," "Adam Bede," and "The Mill on the Floss," celebrated for her psychological depth and mastery of the domestic novel.'

memes.label: Gerald Griffin
memes.description: 'Irish novelist, poet, and playwright (1803–1840), best known for his novel The Collegians, a tale of Irish life.'

memes.label: Gothic Fiction
memes.description: 'A genre of fiction combining romance, horror, and mystery, set in medieval or antiquated settings such as castles and abbeys, inaugurated by Horace Walpole''s "The Castle of Otranto" (1764).'

memes.label: G.R. Gleig
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist, military writer, and chaplain-general to the British Army; author of military and historical novels.'

memes.label: Hannah More
memes.description: 'English religious writer, educator, and philanthropist; author of conduct books and essays on female education who opposed the influence of novels on young readers.'

memes.label: Harriet Beecher Stowe
memes.description: "American novelist and abolitionist; author of Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852), a phenomenally circulated novel that galvanized anti-slavery sentiment."

memes.label: Harriet Martineau
memes.description: 'English social theorist and novelist; used fiction to convey ideas on political economy in the early Victorian period.'

memes.label: Harrison Ainsworth
memes.description: "English historical novelist; author of The Tower of London and Jack Sheppard, who wrote under the influence of Walter Scott."

memes.label: Henry Brooke
memes.description: 'Irish novelist and poet, author of "The Fool of Quality" (1766), a novel of purpose admired by John Wesley and Charles Kingsley for its moral excellence despite its narrative faults.'

memes.label: Henry Fielding
memes.description: 'English novelist and magistrate (1707–1754), author of Tom Jones and a founder of the English novel tradition.'

memes.label: Henry Mackenzie
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist of sensibility; author of The Man of Feeling (1771), a landmark novel of refined sentiment, and The Man of the World, its brutal counterpart.'

memes.label: Herman Melville
memes.description: 'American novelist (1819–1891), author of "Moby-Dick," listed among the notable American writers of fiction in the nineteenth century.'

memes.label: Heroic Romance
memes.description: 'A seventeenth-century genre of lengthy, artificial prose fiction developed by French authors Gomberville, Scudéry, and Calprenède, characterized by martial adventure subordinated to idealized love.'

memes.label: Historical Novel
memes.description: 'A subgenre of fiction that reconstructs past eras through narrative, blending historical fact with imaginative storytelling, most prominently developed by Sir Walter Scott.'

memes.label: Horace Walpole
memes.description: 'English writer, art historian, and Whig politician; author of "The Castle of Otranto" (1764), the first Gothic novel.'

memes.label: Humor in Fiction
memes.description: 'The use of wit, comedy, and humorous characterization in English prose fiction, exemplified by the comic traditions established by Fielding, Smollett, and Sterne in the eighteenth century.'

memes.label: Imagination in Fiction
memes.description: 'The role of the creative imagination in English prose fiction, from the ungoverned fantasies of medieval romance through the allegorical visions of Bunyan to the disciplined imagination of the modern novel.'

memes.label: Literary Imitation
memes.description: 'The practice by which authors deliberately model their works on earlier or foreign literary examples, a recurring force in the development of English prose fiction from the Middle Ages through the nineteenth century.'

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: 'Ireland as a setting and literary region in English prose fiction, particularly through the Irish novels of Edgeworth, Banim, Carleton, and Lever.'

memes.label: Irish Fiction
memes.description: 'The tradition of Irish prose fiction in English, from Edgeworth through the Banim brothers, Carleton, and Lover, depicting Irish life and manners.'

memes.label: Italian Influence on English Fiction
memes.description: 'The extensive literary and cultural influence of Italian tales, writers, and models on the development of English prose fiction from the Elizabethan era onward.'

memes.label: Italy
memes.description: 'Italy as a source of literary influence, setting, and inspiration for English prose fiction from the Renaissance through the nineteenth century.'

memes.label: James Fenimore Cooper
memes.description: 'American novelist who opened a thoroughly national vein in fiction with "The Spy" (1821) and created the Leatherstocking Tales, depicting pioneer and frontier life with patriotic sympathy and original literary power.'

memes.label: James Morier
memes.description: 'British diplomat and novelist, author of the Oriental novel Hajji Baba of Ispahan.'

memes.label: James Payn
memes.description: 'English novelist and editor (1830–1898), author of Lost Sir Massingberd and By Proxy.'

memes.label: Jane Austen
memes.description: 'English novelist; author of "Pride and Prejudice" (1813), "Emma" (1815), and "Sense and Sensibility" (1811), whose studies of country gentry life marked, with Maria Edgeworth, the rise of the female novelist as a major force in English fiction.'

memes.label: Jane Porter
memes.description: 'Scottish-born historical novelist; author of Thaddeus of Warsaw (1803) and The Scottish Chiefs (1810), noted for idealised portrayals of historical figures and events.'

memes.label: John Banim
memes.description: Irish novelist of the early 19th century; known for tales of Irish peasant life

memes.label: John Bunyan
memes.description: 'Puritan preacher and allegorist; author of "The Pilgrim''s Progress" (1678), the greatest allegory in English literature, written during imprisonment for nonconformist preaching.'

memes.label: John Galt
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist; author of Annals of the Parish, noted for humorous depictions of Scottish national character in the tradition of Sir Walter Scott.'

memes.label: John Gibson Lockhart
memes.description: 'Scottish writer and editor, author of the historical novel Valerius.'

memes.label: John Lyly
memes.description: 'Elizabethan prose stylist and dramatist, author of "Euphues, the Anatomy of Wit" (1579), whose ornate and antithetical style gave rise to the term Euphuism.'

memes.label: Jonathan Swift
memes.description: 'Anglo-Irish satirist, essayist, and clergyman, author of Gulliver Travels and A Tale of a Tub, the supreme master of prose satire in English.'

memes.label: Joseph Addison
memes.description: 'English essayist, poet, and statesman; co-founder of "The Spectator" (1711–12) and creator of Sir Roger de Coverley, whose character sketches helped lay the foundation for the English novel.'

memes.label: Lady Georgiana Fullerton
memes.description: 'English novelist (1812–1885) who contributed to the domestic novel tradition, noted for her Catholic themes and religious conversions in fiction.'

memes.label: Lady Morgan
memes.description: 'Irish novelist (c. 1781–1859), born Sydney Owenson, best known for The Wild Irish Girl and for her vivid portrayals of Irish society.'

memes.label: Laurence Sterne
memes.description: 'Anglican clergyman and novelist; author of "The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman" (1759–67), an eccentric and innovative work that broke from the Richardson-Fielding tradition of plotted narrative.'

memes.label: Literary Sketch
memes.description: 'A short prose form that captures a character, scene, or social type in concise, vivid detail, widely practiced throughout the history of English fiction as both a standalone genre and a technique within longer narratives.'

memes.label: London
memes.description: 'The capital of England and a central setting and marketplace for English prose fiction from the 18th century onward.'

memes.label: Margaret Oliphant
memes.description: 'Prolific Scottish Victorian novelist whose works of Scottish life and domestic fiction sustained the interest in regional narrative first established by Sir Walter Scott.'

memes.label: Maria Edgeworth
memes.description: 'Irish novelist and educational writer, pioneer of the regional novel, whose works of Irish life inspired Sir Walter Scott to undertake the Waverley Novels.'

memes.label: Mary Russell Mitford
memes.description: 'English novelist and author of "Our Village"; a popular writer of domestic fiction in the early 19th century.'

memes.label: Mary Shelley
memes.description: 'English novelist (1797–1851), daughter of William Godwin and Mary Wollstonecraft, author of the remarkable but disagreeable Gothic romance "Frankenstein" (1818).'

memes.label: Matthew Lewis
memes.description: 'English Gothic novelist, author of The Monk (1796), whose work carried the Radcliffe school of Gothic fiction to its most sensational extreme.'

memes.label: Middle Ages
memes.description: 'The historical period between the fall of Rome and the Renaissance that gave rise to chivalry, the romance of chivalry, and the foundational narrative traditions of English prose fiction.'

memes.label: Minstrels
memes.description: 'Medieval performers who preserved and transmitted stories, legends, and romances through song and recitation before the rise of written literature.'

memes.label: Mrs. Alexander
memes.description: 'Pseudonym of Annie French Hector (1825–1902), popular English novelist of domestic fiction.'

memes.label: Mrs. Henry Wood
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist, author of the bestselling sensation novel East Lynne.'

memes.label: Nathaniel Hawthorne
memes.description: 'American novelist, the finest writer of fiction New England has produced, whose works including "The Scarlet Letter" and "The House of the Seven Gables" are remarkable for their subtle psychological penetration and imaginative power.'

memes.label: Novel
memes.description: 'The dominant form of extended prose fiction, distinguished from the earlier romance by its realistic portrayal of contemporary life and character.'

memes.label: Novel
memes.description: 'The modern form of prose fiction that evolved from earlier romance traditions, characterized by realistic depiction of life and manners.'

memes.label: Novel of Purpose
memes.description: 'A subgenre of fiction in which the narrative is designed to advance a specific social, political, or moral argument, using storytelling as a vehicle for reform or persuasion.'

memes.label: Oliver Goldsmith
memes.description: 'Irish novelist, poet, and playwright, author of "The Vicar of Wakefield" (1766), celebrated for his exquisite prose style and amiable spirit.'

memes.label: Ouida
memes.description: 'Pseudonym of Marie Louise de la Ramée (1839–1908), prolific English novelist of sensational romances and a leading exponent of the "muscular novel" school.'

memes.label: Oxford
memes.description: 'Oxford as a literary and cultural center, educating generations of English fiction writers from Lyly and Sidney through the nineteenth-century novelists.'

memes.label: Pastoral Romance
memes.description: 'A genre of romance set in an idealized rural world of shepherds and nymphs, blending chivalric conventions with classical pastoral tradition, exemplified by Sir Philip Sidney''s "Arcadia."'

memes.label: Philip Sidney
memes.description: 'Elizabethan courtier, soldier, and poet; author of "Arcadia" (1590), a pastoral romance that marks a foundational moment in English prose fiction.'

memes.label: Puritanism
memes.description: 'The Protestant reform movement of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries whose moral and imaginative influence shaped English prose fiction, most notably through John Bunyan''s "The Pilgrim''s Progress."'

memes.label: Reading Public
memes.description: 'The expanding audience for prose fiction, whose growth in size, diversity, and sophistication fundamentally shaped the development of the English novel from the eighteenth century onward.'

memes.label: Realism in Fiction
memes.description: 'A literary movement and technique emphasizing accurate, detailed portrayal of everyday life and society in prose fiction.'

memes.label: Religious Revival
memes.description: 'The eighteenth-century religious awakening led by Wesley and Whitefield that profoundly influenced English society and left its mark on the moral character of prose fiction.'

memes.label: Rhoda Broughton
memes.description: 'English novelist (1840–1920) of the sensation school, noted for her controversial early works.'

memes.label: Richard Steele
memes.description: 'Irish-born English essayist, playwright, and politician, co-founder of The Tatler (1709) and The Spectator (1711) with Joseph Addison, whose essays shaped Augustan prose style.'

memes.label: Rise of the Novel
memes.description: 'The emergence of the novel as a dominant literary form in 18th-century England, driven by authors like Defoe, Richardson, and Fielding.'

memes.label: Robert Greene
memes.description: 'Elizabethan pamphleteer, poet, and dramatist; author of "Pandosto" which inspired Shakespeare''s "The Winter''s Tale."'

memes.label: Romance of Chivalry
memes.description: A genre of medieval fiction centered on the ideals of knighthood, courtly love, and heroic adventure, forming the earliest foundation of English prose fiction.

memes.label: Romantic Revival
memes.description: 'The late 18th-century literary movement that renewed interest in medieval romance, emotion, and imagination, influencing the development of the Gothic and historical novel.'

memes.label: Round Table
memes.description: 'The legendary table at which King Arthur and his knights assembled, symbolizing the ideals of chivalric equality, fellowship, and honor in medieval English romance, most fully realized in Malory''s "Morte d''Arthur."'

memes.label: Samuel Johnson
memes.description: 'English writer, critic, and lexicographer; author of "Rasselas" (1759), a philosophical novel exploring the search for happiness.'

memes.label: Samuel Lover
memes.description: Irish novelist and songwriter of the early 19th century; known for humorous tales of Irish life

memes.label: Samuel Richardson
memes.description: 'English novelist (1689–1761), author of Pamela and Clarissa, pioneer of the epistolary novel form.'

memes.label: Satire
memes.description: 'A literary mode using wit, irony, and exaggeration to expose human folly and social corruption, mastered by Swift and Fielding.'

memes.label: Scotland
memes.description: 'Scotland as a setting and source of literary tradition in English prose fiction, from Smollett through Scott and Stevenson.'

memes.label: Scottish Fiction
memes.description: 'The tradition of Scottish prose fiction, from Smollett through Scott to Stevenson, distinguished by its regional character, historical themes, and dialect writing.'

memes.label: Sentiment in Fiction
memes.description: 'The expression and cultivation of moral feeling, emotional refinement, and sympathetic sensibility in English prose fiction, particularly prominent in the eighteenth-century novel.'

memes.label: Spain
memes.description: 'Spain as a source of literary influence on English prose fiction, from the chivalric romances through Cervantes to the picaresque tradition.'

memes.label: Susan Ferrier
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist; author of Marriage (1818) and Inheritance (1824), admired by Sir Walter Scott for her natural character studies and lively descriptions of Scottish society.'

memes.label: The Eighteenth-Century Novel
memes.description: 'The growth and perfection of English fiction during the eighteenth century, from Defoe and Swift through Richardson, Fielding, Smollett, and Sterne.'

memes.label: The Nineteenth-Century Novel
memes.description: 'The extraordinary expansion and diversification of English fiction in the nineteenth century, from Scott through the Victorian novelists to the fin de siècle.'

memes.label: The Restoration
memes.description: 'The return of Charles II to the English throne in 1660 and the ensuing period of political and literary transformation that shaped the development of English prose fiction.'

memes.label: Theodore Hook
memes.description: 'English novelist and humorist; author of stories of English domestic life whose uneven literary output reflected the vicissitudes of his personal career.'

memes.label: Thomas Deloney
memes.description: 'Famous ballad-maker of the 16th century and early English novelist; author of "Thomas of Reading."'

memes.label: Thomas Hardy
memes.description: 'English novelist and poet (1840–1928), a major figure in late Victorian and early modern literature noted for the tragic vision of his Wessex novels.'

memes.label: Thomas Holcroft
memes.description: 'Late eighteenth-century English novelist and radical who embodied revolutionary political views in his fiction during the era of the French Revolution.'

memes.label: Thomas Hughes
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist and social reformer best known for "Tom Brown''s School Days," a landmark in English school fiction that appealed to the healthful and manly instincts of boyhood.'

memes.label: Thomas Love Peacock
memes.description: 'English novelist and satirist (1785–1866), author of Headlong Hall and Nightmare Abbey.'

memes.label: Thomas Malory
memes.description: 'Fifteenth-century English knight and author of "Le Morte d''Arthur," the definitive prose compilation of Arthurian chivalric romances that combined the French and English traditions into a single monumental work.'

memes.label: Thomas More
memes.description: 'English lawyer, humanist, and author of "Utopia" (1516), a foundational work of speculative fiction that used the narrative form to advocate political and social reform.'

memes.label: Tobias Smollett
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist and surgeon; author of "Roderick Random" (1748), "Peregrine Pickle" (1751), and "Humphry Clinker" (1771), a major figure in eighteenth-century comic fiction.'

memes.label: Translation in Literature
memes.description: 'The practice of rendering foreign literary works into English, which served as a vital channel for importing continental forms, styles, and ideas into the development of English prose fiction.'

memes.label: Utopia
memes.description: 'A genre of speculative fiction depicting an ideal society, inaugurated in English prose by Sir Thomas More''s "Utopia" (1516) and recurring as a mode of social critique throughout the history of fiction.'

memes.label: Walter Scott
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist and poet (1771–1832), creator of the historical novel and central figure of the Romantic Revival.'

memes.label: Washington Irving
memes.description: 'American author, essayist, and historian; one of the first American writers to achieve international fame, known for "Rip Van Winkle" and "The Sketch Book."'

memes.label: Wilkie Collins
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist and pioneer of sensation fiction, known for "The Woman in White" and "The Moonstone"; also wrote historical fiction including "Antonina."'

memes.label: William Beckford
memes.description: 'English novelist, art collector, and patron; author of Vathek (1786), an oriental romance composed in three days and praised by Byron as superior to Rasselas.'

memes.label: William Black
memes.description: 'Scottish novelist of the late 19th century, noted for Scottish regional fiction.'

memes.label: William Carleton
memes.description: 'Irish novelist; author of "Traits and Stories of the Irish Peasantry" depicting Irish rural life'

memes.label: William Godwin
memes.description: 'English political philosopher and novelist (1756–1836), author of "Caleb Williams" (1794), deeply influenced by the French Revolution; husband of Mary Wollstonecraft and father of Mary Shelley.'

memes.label: William Makepeace Thackeray
memes.description: 'Victorian novelist and satirist; author of "Vanity Fair" (1847–48), one of the central works of English fiction known for its moral vision and rich characterization.'
